speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you want me to slate it. OK, usually-- OK, yeah. Oh, OK. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do I say? This is tape three with Leon Wendel. [INAUDIBLE] OK. This is probably going to go to five. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- yeah, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am not a believer in cutting down.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, nor am I.

speaker: interviewee
text: I doubt that we'll be able for lunch-- or, we'll be finished for lunch with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll be finished-- whenever we're finished, it'll be lunch time. [LAUGHS] Don't worry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is tape three with Leon Wendel. Tape three, on April 24th, 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just lost a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't that good a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about when the order came in to form the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So they-- they made plans, and they said [NON-ENGLISH] are going to be in the ghetto. And they said the people should move into the ghetto. And people started moving all their belongings in carts by hand. Horses were taken away. Transportation was very limited. People were pulling carts with certain furnitures and things out of the areas which is not-- which was not going to be the ghetto. My uncle-- my oldest uncle, my father's brother, older than my father-- he lived in the area, but was not supposed to have been in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Shlomo. And-- and our building was also a shtiebel. A shtiebel is-- you could call it a temple. Like the orthodox, you know, they-- there was a temple and there were shtiebels. Those are different. A shtiebel was just a room where they got together and they prayed every day. In our neighborhood, must have been 100 shtiebels. There were only two big temples-- I mean, real big temples. And the population, no-- it wasn't even possible for them to-- there wasn't even space enough for them. There were a lot of shtiebels. And of our-- in our building was a shtiebel. It was a big room, maybe twice as long as this one. And when the war broke out and my uncle had to move and we were the owners-- so we gave him the key and he moved into the shtiebel the whole family. And he lived there. And then, they made the ghetto, and Poles moved-- or Germans moved into that area where he lived. But his was on the edge of the ghetto. And then they changed their mind, and his fell back into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he moved back into the ghetto. It was a dragging twice. It was a mess. And some of his family went out from Lodz. They-- the relatives-- they had relatives in smaller places, or the ones which was going to be what they called the general government.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was supposed to be the protectorate of Poland. So they figure it's going to be better to live in a Polish protectorate than in the Third Reich-- than in the German Reich. So some of his family went away there. Now, my brother, Hershel, his-- here, you would say girlfriend-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. Her parents were in a small town. Her stepmother-- her mother wasn't alive-- her stepmother, my uncle's wife-- she was a cousin of my-- I would call her Sura. Her name was Sura. My brother, Hershel's future wife, name was Sura-- she got stuck with us in Lodz. And since she lived there where they're supposed to have been-- become outside the ghetto, she moved in the-- in with us. She was-- she was my mother's brother's daughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when-- when they changed it-- when they changed it back, either she didn't have where to move in-- there was nobody there. I don't know what the reason was, but she had remained with us, and she lived with us. Now, we lived in two big rooms. There were eight people. She was the ninth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when it was decided that this part is going to be the ghetto and her parents were not in Lodz, and she lived in our house, my father said, she cannot stay here unless she's married. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we made what you call a [NON-ENGLISH], just a marriage ceremony. And that's how she stayed with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she married your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then slowly, the ghetto was closed. Every day were new proclamation of this and proclamation of restrictions of that. The Jews don't have a right to bear witness. The Jews don't have a right to go to court. The Jews have no right of this, and no-- you couldn't ride a streetcar, and like that. You couldn't go to a movie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother had his place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He rented a room, or-- Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother, Leibel, where he worked his furniture shop. And when the war came, that month of-- before the war in Europe, he paid rent by the quarry. That quarry wasn't paid yet. So whatever furniture he had there, we took it home to us. The room was emptied.

speaker: interviewee
text: So his landlady-- she was German, from Poland-- German. Volksdeutsche. She came over to our house, and she said she doesn't want the money. The money is not worth nothing. So there was a dining set which was finished completely, except the finish, the [NON-ENGLISH], was done by hand. The-- the shine, you know, how would you call this? The veneer.

speaker: interviewee
text: The veneer was on, but just the finish, just the shine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was worth maybe 600, 700 zloty. And she took that. She said that's what she wants for the rent. The rent was maybe 40 zloty or 30, you understand. And I remember my father saying, you take it. And after she left, he says, wait, wait until this war is over. We're going to show her. We're going to take her. You know, she's not going to get away with that. Of course, she didn't get away with that. She-- she took it and that was the end of it. And then they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they closed the ghetto on May 1st, 1940. I remember this. And after that, the ghetto started to get organized. In the beginning, it was a chaos. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, we were all home already by that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my-- my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brother was already married. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So there were nine people in two rooms. And this was good, because people were much worse off than that, much more crowded than that. [INAUDIBLE] was a crowded place in the first place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then her-- my wife's brother came home, and there was nobody there because they all ran along with the Polish when the army said to come. They ran away. So he went with us. He's also a cousin of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan [? Newsome. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the wall was closed. I don't know how we lived or how we managed. It was chaotic. It wasn't-- what you could bring home was all right. But in the beginning, there was plenty of food. There was a, like, a [NON-ENGLISH]. You could buy all you wanted if you had the money. The mark was very strong. And we didn't have many marks to begin with. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it went on like this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a while-- not for too long. The ghetto was organized real fast-- too fast. The Jews have a very big knack for organizing. They set up organizations in-- in no time. The Germans selected the [NON-ENGLISH] of the Juden, which means the oldest of the Jew. It has two meanings. I mean, he happened to be an old man. But the meaning didn't mean because he's old. It means he is the [NON-ENGLISH], the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Chaim [? Romkowski. ?] He was-- he was running [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, for children, a-- children without parents-- orphanage before the war. He was not a man off the street. He was, you know, he was-- he was an [? old ?] person. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have seen him many times. I did not know him, you know. I never came to-- to speak to him. I was just a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during the day in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll come to that. I didn't do nothing. I did what-- we did-- my father was still working in his own little shop, making a few marks here, a few marks there. I paid him a little this. I paid him-- And while the committee, or the [NON-ENGLISH], was organizing, they set up some resorts, what they called, which means a place to work. It's not a resort [INAUDIBLE]. Shops, resort shops. And they needed people to help set up. And my father would go and fix some machine or do little things, you know, which had to be done, for himself. And the people who ran that thing, the committee, they could pay out a certain amount of money to private persons. And then they put people to work for the German army. That started out right in the beginning. And people started to plant little plots with greens and things. And I was helping my father, you know-- carry things. There was no transportation, you know. You had to deliver something, you had to carry it. I used to go along and I had to hold, let's say, a piece of metal right here [INAUDIBLE] or something or whatever he did. Or-- so this is what I did. And then the ghetto got organized. We used to joke about it that they, in a year, the ghetto is going to run like a clock, on clockwork, that perfect. And it ran like clockwork. And everybody was slowly forced to go and work into the ghetto. As more things got worse, you couldn't buy things. Then they started rationing. And if we didn't have, let's say, a card-- you didn't have a job, you couldn't get a rationing card. Everybody had to register and have a Kennkarte card, which means an identification card. So they knew exactly how many people there was. And they took your name. They took what-- oh, you have any trade or you didn't have a trade, how old you are, and all that. And-- and we had a newspaper coming out, the [? Ghettozeit. ?] Not for too long because they ran out of paper. Of course, it had no news outside. What was-- it tells you how to do certain things, how to try to cut corners, and advice things, or things which were happening. Like you had to come here, you had to come there. You have to register this or register that. You had to give up, let's say, [INAUDIBLE] or jewelry or a radio if somebody had, or guns, or you had to turn in. They tell you where to go, what to do. Then they started putting out proclamations on-- on the street. In the beginning, if I remember right, they were in three languages-- Polish, German, and Yiddish. Then the Polish was cut out and it was just Yiddish and-- and German. It was always signed by [? Romkowski. ?] He was the [NON-ENGLISH] of the Juden. Now, he had a committee. And the committee was divided, like, one is taking care on the vegetables. One is taking care on work. One is taking care on all kinds of things which was going on. And slowly, those committees expanded that then they became more and more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was no way avoiding having to go to them, or be registered with them. And there was no other way. I mean, if you lived in an apartment, you had to register. How many is in the apartment? How big is the apartment? And so on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we owned the building. We had no say over nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The electric bill was not paid by nobody. The city or the committee paid for the whole ghetto, whatever, if they paid. And so on and on, like water and things. All-- nothing was paid. Whatever was repaid had to go through them. Things were deteriorating. Nothing was painted or anything. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't work for a long time. My father said, whatever you do for your-- he had the philosophy. Whatever you do for yourself, you're always going to be ahead than doing it for somebody else, or working for somebody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- we avoided it as long as we could. My oldest brother Leibel, in the army, he was-- he took a course in the fire brigade. He was artillery, but he also took a course in the fire brigade. So in the ghetto, they said we need a fire department.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the oldest. And he was very aggressive. He was an aggressive person from nature. So he joined the fire brigade. It wasn't so easy to get in, but he had a paper from the army that he finished a course in fire protection or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had already somebody in the committee, which was a big help. He could-- from the resource that he was rationed, he could bring home some pieces of wood, which was a big thing in ghetto. There was no delivery, things like that. He got an extra soup, which was a big thing. Before, it was strictly rationed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but he was also good furniture making, and he was known in town. So when they made the furniture factory, he had to go work in the furniture factory. So he was in the voluntary fire department, in the furniture factory, in the same factory he worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every day he came home, he bought some wood. He-- can you see my foot? He-- you know, we wore those-- to hold down the socks, those rubber things. Garters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Garters. So he would put around pieces of wood, put the garter on it, put the piece of wood [INAUDIBLE] and let it down, and come home like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he would smuggle in pieces of wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a big thing. I mean, the winters in Poland are really cold. And-- and when you don't eat enough, they're even colder. So-- and he was a big help. My other brother-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the hunger was very big. It didn't take long. I mean, in the beginning, it was enough food to be satisfied. Meat was right away no good. There was some horse meat, which we refused to eat in the beginning. And then when we were hungry enough to eat it, there wasn't anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother, Manny, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from upstairs, you know, he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he got-- he did something. He had a fight with somebody in a line for some oil-- a little oil they were handing out. So they arrested him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once he got arrested, they had already a record. Now, in ghetto, there were some disturbances-- like, some strikes or something-- and demonstrations. I'm talking about 1940, right after the closing of the ghetto. So they registered the people. And then the Germans came, and they said they need people to go to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in order to supply the people what they needed to work-- let's say they needed 200, 300 people-- they picked the strong arm people. In other words, they knew who the strong arm people from before the war who were-- you know, we used to call them-- it wasn't-- the word wasn't strong arm, but the word was-- well, it would be considered here like a hoodlum.

speaker: interviewee
text: But hoodlum there and hoodlum here is still a big difference, you know. They didn't have guns or-- or anything like that. They didn't kill people. They could beat up somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they arrested those people. And this was already arrested by the Jewish Sonderkommando, the Jewish police, inside the ghetto. They created the Jewish police. And my brother being-- had been in a fight, he was arrested with them. And he was sent away with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1940 or '41. I am not absolutely sure. I think it was in 1941. And he went-- he was sent to a camp, to a working camp. And little by little, the ghetto got rid of all the troublemakers. Like, people who got up and made speeches about this and about that, they were all sent out from the ghetto into labor camps. And the ghetto was, how would you say it, kept docile or made quiet. You know, there was no resistance. Now, the ghetto was ringed around with barbed wire. Being in the middle of the city and not having any big fields open, is what they put a wire on one side of the street. Then there was the street itself, and a wire, and the opposite sidewalk. So there was, like, a no man's land in between. So if you-- to get out, in other words, you had to go through two fences. And in that middle, no man's land, around maybe every 100 yards, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] within seeing distance from each other, was a soldier with-- with a gun. And they put up those little houses, you know, like a soldier to be inside when it snows or rains-- like a watch house, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a booth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a booth, right. And there were the soldiers there. And in the beginning, we walked along those sidewalks. Then the Germans started shooting just for the fun of it. And people got shot just trying to walk on-- on the sidewalk which was right near the fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people didn't walk anymore those sidewalks. But from yards to yards were made holes in walls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it became like, if you wanted to go from one street to the other, you didn't even have to go to the street. You went from this yard to this yard to this yard to this yard. So this also became a way of life. Then on the main streets, which were about two main streets, they put up a bridge-- a wooden bridge. And the people had to cross over that bridge to go from one part of the ghetto to the other, because the ghetto was dissected into two pieces. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And one time, I remember, a tenant of ours from before the war, you know. And his son crossed it, had to go at night. He worked. He had to be early, you know. It was still dark. And the guard down there shot him off the bridge. He got killed right there. And it happened more than once. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we avoided those streets. But sometimes you had to cross. You have no other choice, you know. Like, you had to go to the soup kitchen and the kitchen was on the other side, and you had to cross. You took a chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: So sometimes, we knew-- we used to say, [NON-ENGLISH], which means the red one, you know, is standing guard. Then the whole-- nobody crossed that bridge while he is on duty because we knew he is shooting. We already could recognize some. And there were times which it was known you could walk and nobody bothered you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were certain times there. And after a time, they tried to force everybody to work in one of those factories. My brother worked in a furniture factory. The hunger got so bad that when they called for people to go to work, they never called it any other way, you know. It was always called that you're going to be resettled into a working place-- you know, not resettled to the east or whatever. Resettled to a working place. Then there was never where or what. My next brother, my brother, Mendel, volunteered. And he was sent out, and he did go to a working place, like my brother, Manny, before him. And he went to Posen, in the city of Posen. We got mail a couple of times-- Posen stadium. There was mail in the ghetto at the beginning. And the mail was sporadic-- sometimes yes and sometimes no. You addressed the letter to [? back, ?] to the [NON-ENGLISH] of the Juden, Litzmannstadt ghetto. Then you gave your address.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Litzmannstadt was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Litzmannstadt was the German name. They changed the name into Litzmannstadt, from Lodz into Litzmannstadt. Litzmannstadt was the name of the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one time, I even remember that we sent a package to my brother, Manny, to his camp, from [INAUDIBLE]. Food we didn't have, from [INAUDIBLE]. But I think this was only one time you could send something out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the hunger was so big, and my brother, Hershel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who got married, she got pregnant. And they-- it was whispered behind my ear. I was just a boy. For me, it was hidden, you know. So-- but I knew. And he volunteered. He

speaker: interviewee
text: Said, what chance do we have here? It can't be any worse than here. You've got to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were sent out. Now I know that they went-- they went straight to the [NON-ENGLISH] camp, or destruction camp, or whatever it was called. It was called [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] in Polish, not too far from Lodz. And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this most have been in 1941 yet. He didn't work in a resort. By 1942, I think we all worked. Maybe it was '40-- late in '41 or something like that, or beginning of '42. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the trains were taking the people. There was a train going every day with a few hundred people-- 500, 600, 700 people. And the trains came back in the evening or next morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to change to the next tape now.
